EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge of surface and upper level high pressure parked itself over much of the eastern United States, resulting in widespread extreme heat and humidity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three days of high heat and humidity brought extreme heat indices. The heat index peaked at 109 degrees on June 23rd, 105 degrees on June 24th and 106 degrees on June 25th, all measured at McGuire Air Force Base.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A progressive low pressure system emerged out of the Tennessee Valley and quickly moved across the region bringing an accumulating wet snowfall. Most places received an advisory level snowfall with mainly 3 to 4 inches of snow, with some locations getting slightly higher and lower amounts. This was a front end quick thump driven by warm advection snowfall which fell primarily during the evening hours of the 8th. The snow quickly tapered to some light freezing rain and drizzle in spots after the initial thump.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Accumulating snow occurred over a few hours during the mid to late evening before transitioning to light mixed precipitation with some light freezing rain and drizzle during a portion of the overnight. Trained spotters reported 3.1 inches in Wallington and 2.9 inches in East Rutherford. Cocorahs observers reported 3.0 inches at 1.8 ESE Montvale and 3.0 inches in North Arlington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A progressive low pressure system emerged out of the Tennessee Valley and quickly moved across the region bringing an accumulating wet snowfall. Most places received an advisory level snowfall with mainly 3 to 4 inches of snow, with some locations getting slightly higher and lower amounts. This was a front end quick thump driven by warm advection snowfall which fell primarily during the evening hours of the 8th. The snow quickly tapered to some light freezing rain and drizzle in spots after the initial thump.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An accumulating snow occurred during the mid to late evening before transitioning to a wintry mix with a period of light freezing rain / drizzle for a portion of the overnight. Cocorahs reported 3.0 inches in Fair Lawn and 3.0 inches in Oakland. The public reported 2.6 inches in Franklin Lakes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed through, with a secondary cold frontal boundary lingering back to the west. Low pressure formed along the boundary and began to strengthen as it pulled away quickly to the east and northeast. The accumulating snow was generated mainly from frontogenetical forcing which occurred to the north and west of the developing surface low. Winter Storm criteria was just missed across Western Passaic, with the remainder of Northeast New Jersey achieving advisory criteria, or falling just short of advisory criteria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cocorahs observers reported 5.6 inches in Little Falls, 4.8 inches in Wayne, 4.2 inches in Hawthorne. The public reported 5.0 inches in Totowa and 4.8 inches in Passaic.  Snow amounts on average reached advisory level criteria.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A damage survey was conducted at a home on N Blue Bell Rd near the intersecting street of Coles Mill Rd where a concentrated amount of damage occurred. It was determined that a brief gustnado formed at this location, snapping the trunk of several hardwood trees and taking down several large softwood branches. Video received from the homeowner showed the trees and branches falling after a rapid wind shift. Analysis of radar data showed a strong signature of a rear-flank downdraft in the vicinity, which likely spun up this brief gustnado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front over the region resulted in two rounds of severe thunderstorms. The first was in the late morning through mid day. After a few hours lull, a second round of thunderstorms occurred late in the afternoon through the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado originated in Williamstown near the intersection of Janvier Rd and Tuckahoe Rd where a large hardwood tree fell across Janvier Rd and some siding from the corner of a house peeled off. The tornado then moved SSE crossing Birch Ave where several hardwood trees were snapped at the trunks. This is where the tornado reached its greatest width of 300 yards. Traveling SE from there, where it likely lifted at times, the tornado crossed W Malaga Rd near the Victory Lakes community, damaging a wood fence and several small tree branches. Continuing its discontinuous path SE, a telephone pole was snapped near the intersection of E Piney Hollow Rd and US 322 before moving SSE again towards the Collings Lakes neighborhood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front over the region resulted in two rounds of severe thunderstorms. The first was in the late morning through mid day. After a few hours lull, a second round of thunderstorms occurred late in the afternoon through the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The first damage in Collings Lakes was noted along Malaga Road where large branches were downed and some metal roof sheeting was peeled off an out building. Continued damage, mostly large branches downed continued near the intersection of Cains Mill Road and Colton Lane. The last observed damage in this path was near the intersection of |Cloverdale Lane and Wiltshire Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal trough approached at the surface out ahead of a cold front. Aloft, a shortwave trough led to falling heights into the afternoon. These features combined with MLCAPE values between 1000 and 2000 J/kg and moderate shear allowed for thunderstorms to develop and become organized. These storms produced damaging wind gusts and frequent cloud to ground lightning in northeast NJ.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree on house in Cranford NJ.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storm surge from Hurricane Erin and an upcoming New Moon resulted in moderate to locally major high tide levels on the night of August 21, 2025 across coastal areas of New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread roadway flooding occurred in the tidal communities of Monmouth County. Water entered vulnerable buildings causing some damage. The tide gauge at Sea Bright reached 6.59 feet MLLW, the tide gauge at Belmar reached 7.12 feet MLLW, and the tide gauge at Manasquan reached 6.74 feet MLLW.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light winds and a moist air mass ahead of an approaching cold front, combined with clearing skies and recent rainfall, allowed dense fog to form across parts of southern New Jersey on the early morning of August 25th. The fog dissipated shortly after sunrise.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several hours of dense fog was observed at Ocean County Airport.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple large branches downed. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front sparked off showers and thunderstorms across the forecast area. Flash flooding resulted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of flash flooding were received in Keansburg on Belleview and Center Avenues.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple days of onshore flow had resulted in elevated rip current risk across the region. There had been a moderate or high risk from July 31 through August 12. Several area beach patrols reported a much higher than average number of rescues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven swimmers were caught in a rip current at the Webster Avenue beach. Rescuers attempted to rescue all of the affected people. Six were rescued. Of those six, three had to be transported to a hospital to recover. The seventh individual was found partially submerged in the water. CPR was administered on the beach, and he was taken to a nearby hospital where he was pronounced dead.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously hot air mass overspread Northeastern New Jersey on June 23, 2025 as high pressure was centered over the Ohio and Tennessee Valleys. Heat index values peaked at around 110 degrees F in the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on ASOS station KTEB, Teterboro Airport in Teterboro, New Jersey, heat index values in nearby locations reached extreme heat criteria values of at least 105 degrees F from approximately 930 AM through 1830 PM Local Standard Time. Media reported that approximately 150 attendees of two outdoor high school graduation ceremonies during the late morning into early afternoon for the Paterson, NJ school district were treated for heat exhaustion, with another 16 sent to the hospital for treatment of more serious levels of heat exhaustion.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep layered troughing approached the area from the west along with a cold front at the surface. The area was able to destabilize ahead of the cold front to between 1000 and 1500 j/kg of MLCAPE. The front sparked showers and thunderstorms and with 30 to 40 kt of Effective Bulk Shear available, many of the storms became strong to severe. Single cells and multi-cell clusters were able to produce damaging wind gusts in northeast NJ.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree fallen on car on Myrtle Avenue near Mariners Place with 2 occupants deceased.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep layered troughing approached the area from the west along with a cold front at the surface. The area was able to destabilize ahead of the cold front to between 1000 and 1500 j/kg of MLCAPE. The front sparked showers and thunderstorms and with 30 to 40 kt of Effective Bulk Shear available, many of the storms became strong to severe. Single cells and multi-cell clusters were able to produce damaging wind gusts in northeast NJ.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees down on Hillside Avenue, including a large one on a home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: At the surface, a cold front slowly approached from the west, with a pre-frontal trough developing ahead of it. Aloft, the area remained generally on the eastern edge of mid/upper level troughing. While shear was not all that impressive (about 25 kt of 0-6km bulk shear), strong heating aided in MLCAPE values reaching about 2000 j/kg. With lift from the aforementioned features, and likely help from the right entrance region of an upper level jet streak, showers and thunderstorms started to develop. Some storms reached severe criteria and produce damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree and wires down in West Milford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful coastal storm sat off the New Jersey coast, with strong winds pushing water towards the shoreline and into the back bays of New Jersey. Moderate to locally major high tide levels were observed along the coast. Tidal flooding continued over multiple high tide cycles.

The tide gauge at Sea Bright reached 6.97 feet MLLW, the tide gauge at Belmar reached 7.45 feet MLLW, the tide gauge at Manasquan reached 7.00 feet MLLW, the tide gauge at Keansburg reached 8.0 feet MLLW, and the tide gauge at Sandy Hook reached 7.71 feet MLLW.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A progressive shortwave from the west overran a cold high pressure system resulting in a significant snowfall across the region. Warm advection aloft was the main driver of the snowfall for much of the event. An accumulating snowfall occurred across Northeastern New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cocorahs observers reported 3.8 inches at 0.6 WNW West Orange Township, 3.5 inches in Montclair, 3.5 inches in Maplewood, and 3.5 inches in Verona. The public reported 3.3 inches at 1 W Essex Fells and 3.8 inches at 1 S Roseland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A progressive shortwave from the west overran a cold high pressure system resulting in a significant snowfall across the region. Warm advection aloft was the main driver of the snowfall for much of the event. An accumulating snowfall occurred across Northeastern New Jersey.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm air lifting above antecedent cold and moist air resulted in dense fog across portions of west-central New Jersey starting just before midnight on December 29th. The fog dissipated as the front lifted north towards noon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several hours of dense fog was reported at Trenton-Mercer Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful cold front brought strong winds to eastern New Jersey on the evening of December 29, 2025.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The USGS gauge in Cape May Harbor reported a wind gust of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm accompanied by strong warm advection aloft brought a widespread wintry mix of precipitation to portions of the region. A coating of snow and sleet, as well as a coating of freezing rain was reported across much of eastern Pennsylvania, New Jersey, and northern Delmarva. The heaviest totals were reported across northern New Jersey, where as much as 4 of snow fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trace amounts of sleet totals were reported. A light glaze of freezing rain/freezing drizzle also occurred across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Advisory and some Warning level snowfall occurred across NE New Jersey. An quasi arctic / polar boundary moved across the region as elongated weak low pressure formed along the boundary and moved along the boundary to the east northeast. The region was on the cold side of the boundary which resulted in accumulating snowfall region wide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Cocorahs observer reported 5.9 inches in Little Falls. The public reported 5.0 inches at 1 NE Wayne, and 4.5 inches at 1 N Wayne. A trained spotter reported 5.0 inches in Hawthorne.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front passed through northeastern New Jersey during the afternoon of December 19, 2025. The pressure gradient tightened in response, and produced high winds during the early evening hours on this day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 642 PM Local Standard Time, a high wind speed gust of 58 mph was recorded at mesonet station Robbins Reef Lighthouse in Bayonne, NJ, at an elevation of 69 feet above ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wintry system brought widespread moderate rainfall to the region, and the first accumulating snowfall, sleet, and ice of the season to portions of northern New Jersey and eastern Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snowfall, sleet, and a glaze of ice occurred. Snowfall totals ranged from 1-3. The highest snowfall report was 3.6 from Kittatinny Lake. 0.03 of ice was reported at the Sussex Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wintry system brought widespread moderate rainfall to the region, and the first accumulating snowfall, sleet, and ice of the season to portions of northern New Jersey and eastern Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sleet and a light glaze of ice occurred. Sleet accumulation was minimal, but nearby reports suggest as much as 0.5 of sleet may have occurred in the far northern portions of the county. Trace amounts were reported in Holland Township and Flemington. No ice measurement reports were received, but nearby reports and analysis indicate at least a trace occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A convective line of showers developed ahead of a rapidly approaching cold front, leading to isolated damaging convective winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple large tree limbs were blown down on a house leading to roof damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense band of showers and thunderstorms developed just ahead of an approaching occluded front. One of the storms was responsible for a microburst in Salem County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst occurred in the Lennox Run development in Pedricktown. The most significant damage occurred at a residence on Clara Drive where a pontoon boat was blown off a trailer and into a wood fence, doors to a shed were damaged, and a portion of fascia board was blown off. The homeowner noted damage visible on the interior of the outer walls on the second floor, though there wasn't any visible damage on the exterior of the wall. No damage was noted outside of this development. The estimated maximum wind speed was determined with the damage to the fascia board.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Fire departments responded to a swimmer in distress on Tuesday, September 2 at a Lavallette Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fire departments from multiple municipalities responded to a report of a swimmer in distress at the New York Avenue Beach in Lavallette.  The individual was found partially submerged in the ocean about 25 yards from shore. He was taken to a local medical center where he was pronounced dead. Most beaches in the area are unguarded after Labor Day.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow and sleet totals ranged from 2-4. The highest report was 4.5 from Green Pond and Rockaway. A light glaze of freezing rain/freezing drizzle was also reported across the county, which lingered into the morning hours on the 27th.
